scenario.title: Triple Alarm — One Trigger Is Enough
scenario.narrative: An alarm fires when ANY of three independent sensors trips. Build the condition-coverage table for the four submitted tests and decide whether Branch Condition coverage is achieved.

claim: Branch Condition coverage is 100% with the four tests below.

options.label: Yes — every condition takes both T and F at least once.
No — at least one condition is missing a T or F value.

hints: Branch Condition coverage requires that EACH condition (A, B, C) be observed as TRUE in at least one test AND as FALSE in at least one test. Re-read Law Card 4.
Build the table column by column. A: T in T1, F in T2/T3/T4 ✓. B: T in T2, F in T1/T3/T4 ✓. C: T in T3, F in T1/T2/T4 ✓.
Solution — YES, BC is 100%. But notice: BCC would still need 2³=8 combinations (only 4 of 8 are present). BC ≠ BCC — that distinction is the next layer up (§5.3.5).

seeded_faults.description: Sensor C is silently OR-ed in only when A is also true (a typo bug), but Branch Condition coverage cannot detect this.

misconceptions.explanation_md: BC coverage is achieved when each condition is observed as both T and F across the test set — independent of how many combinations are tested. This case does satisfy BC, but BC still does NOT detect interaction bugs between conditions: that requires BCC (Law Card 5) or MCDC (Law Card 6).
